mnemonics.title: RTOS CHARACTERISTICS
mnemonics.content:
R - RESPONSE (Predictable timing)
T - TASK (Handles multiple tasks)
O - OPERATES (Operates under constraints)
S - SCHEDULING (Priority-based scheduling)

mnemonics.title: EMBEDDED OS FEATURES
mnemonics.content:
LIGHT - Lightweight code
TINY - Minimal footprint
SPECIFIC - Designed for specific hardware
CONSTRAINED - Limited resources
REAL-TIME - Often has timing constraints
DEDICATED - Single-purpose devices

acronyms.usage: Used in systems requiring guaranteed response time like control systems, aerospace
Multiple identical processors sharing memory, used in servers and high-performance systems
Master-slave processor model, used in embedded systems and specific hardware control
Dynamic scheduling algorithm that prioritizes tasks with earliest deadline
Fixed-priority scheduling where shorter period tasks get higher priority
Standard API for Unix-like systems, with real-time extensions (POSIX.1b)
Memory access time depends on memory location relative to processor
Network of physical devices with embedded systems collecting and sharing data

analogies.analogy: Like a traffic light controller - must change lights within milliseconds regardless of other system activities. Missing a deadline (delaying a light change) could cause accidents.
Like a thermostat - performs one specific task continuously, has minimal interface, operates with limited resources, and must respond reliably without human intervention.
Like a franchise restaurant chain - multiple locations (computers) work independently but share recipes (resources) and appear as one brand to customers (users).
Like a team of identical workers sharing one desk and tools - anyone can do any task, and work is divided equally among available workers.
Like a factory assembly line - each worker (processor) has a specific job: supervisor gives orders, others perform dedicated tasks.

visualTips.visualization: Think of a timeline: HARD REAL-TIME = airplane landing - MUST land by deadline or catastrophe. SOFT REAL-TIME = video buffering - frame drops are annoying but not fatal.
Draw two boxes: SMP shows 4 equal boxes connected to one memory (all equal), AMP shows one big box (master) connected to three smaller boxes (slaves).

Imagine a parking lot: high-priority car (task) waits while low-priority car holds the only exit - this causes priority inversion. Solution: priority inheritance temporarily boosts the low-priority car's priority.

Draw layers: MONOLITHIC = one big block with everything inside. MICROKERNEL = small core with separate boxes (services) stacked around it - easier to fix problems in one service without breaking the whole system.
Use a Gantt chart showing time slots with task bars of different lengths. EDF: shortest deadline bar goes next. RMS: bar with shortest repeat interval gets priority at each slot.
